title: Imatinib treatment of CML through BCR-ABL inhibition
abstract: Imatinib (Gleevec) is a tyrosine kinase inhibitor that effectively treats chronic myeloid leukemia (CML) by specifically inhibiting the BCR-ABL fusion protein. The BCR-ABL fusion gene results from the Philadelphia chromosome translocation t(9;22). Imatinib binds to the ATP-binding site of BCR-ABL, blocking its constitutive kinase activity and thereby preventing proliferation of CML cells. This targeted therapy has dramatically improved outcomes for CML patients and serves as a paradigm for molecularly targeted cancer therapy. Imatinib also inhibits the KIT receptor tyrosine kinase and PDGFR. Resistance to Imatinib can occur through mutations in the BCR-ABL kinase domain, such as the T315I mutation.

title: Checkpoint inhibitors in melanoma: PD-1 and CTLA-4 pathways
abstract: Immune checkpoint inhibitors have revolutionized the treatment of metastatic melanoma. PD-1 (Programmed cell death protein 1) is an immune checkpoint receptor expressed on T cells. Its ligand PD-L1 is often upregulated on tumor cells to evade immune destruction. Pembrolizumab and Nivolumab are monoclonal antibodies that block PD-1 interaction with PD-L1, thereby reactivating T cell-mediated tumor killing. CTLA-4 is another immune checkpoint that downregulates T cell activation. Ipilimumab targets CTLA-4 and was the first immune checkpoint inhibitor approved for melanoma. Combination therapy with Nivolumab and Ipilimumab has shown superior response rates compared to either agent alone, though with increased immune-related adverse events. The BRAF V600E mutation is common in melanoma and can be targeted by BRAF inhibitors such as Vemurafenib and Dabrafenib, often used in combination with MEK inhibitors like Trametinib.
